content:
# Galatians Chapter 3 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.1 — "publicly portrayed as crucified" — rendering and expansion

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "before whose eyes Yeshua Christos was publicly portrayed [as] crucified" | proegraphē — "publicly displayed/written before" |
| LOGOS | "It was before your eyes that Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified" | Same; restructured for English |
| PNEUMA | "the meaning of Jesus Christ's death was made as clear to you as if you had seen him die on the cross with your own eyes" | Full expansion; "meaning...made clear...as if you had seen" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. proegraphē estaurōmenos means "publicly displayed as crucified." PNEUMA expands into a simile ("as if you had seen him die") and adds "the meaning of" — going well beyond the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands a vivid verb into a full simile.

---

### v.2 — "hearing of faith" vs. "hearing with faith" vs. "believed the message you heard about Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from hearing of faith" | akoēs pisteōs — genitive construction |
| LOGOS | "by hearing with faith" | "With faith" — hearing accompanied by faith |
| PNEUMA | "because you believed the message you heard about Christ" | Full paraphrase; adds "about Christ" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. akoēs pisteōs is a compressed genitive — "hearing of faith" could mean "the message that produces faith," "hearing characterized by faith," or "the hearing that faith involves." LOGOS's "hearing with faith" preserves the connection without over-specifying. PNEUMA unpacks into a full clause and adds "about Christ."

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. akoēs pisteōs — the relationship between hearing and faith is a key Pauline concept. Is faith the content of what is heard, the response to what is heard, or the mode of hearing? The genitive is deliberately polyvalent.

---

### v.3 — "completed by the flesh" vs. "perfected by the flesh" vs. "perfect by your own human effort"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "being completed by [the] flesh" | epiteleisthe — "bring to completion" |
| LOGOS | "being perfected by the flesh" | "Perfected" — standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "trying to become perfect by your own human effort" | "Trying to become perfect"; "human effort" for sarx |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. epiteleō means "bring to completion/perfection." PNEUMA adds "trying to become" (not in Greek — the verb is passive) and glosses sarx ("flesh") as "your own human effort" — an interpretation, not a translation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes passive to active and interprets sarx.

---

### v.8 — "proclaimed the gospel beforehand" vs. "preached the gospel beforehand" vs. "God proclaimed this Good News...long ago"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "proclaimed the gospel beforehand to Abraham" | proeuēngelisato — compound verb |
| LOGOS | "preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "God proclaimed this Good News to Abraham long ago when he said" | Adds "long ago when he said"; identifies subject as God |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The subject of proeuēngelisato is "the Scripture" (hē graphē), not God. PNEUMA changes the subject. Both EMET and LOGOS correctly render the Scripture as the agent of the pre-gospel.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes the subject from "Scripture" to "God."

---

### v.10 — "as many as are from works of law" vs. "all who rely on works of the law" vs. "those who depend on the law to make them right with God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as many as are from works of law" | hosoi ex ergōn nomou — literal |
| LOGOS | "all who rely on works of the law" | "Rely on" — contextual |
| PNEUMA | "those who depend on the law to make them right with God" | "Depend on...to make them right with God" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The idiom "from works of law" (ex ergōn nomou) means those whose identity and standing are based on law observance — "rely on" captures this. PNEUMA adds "to make them right with God" — an interpretive expansion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds purpose clause.

---

### v.13 — "having become a curse for us" vs. "becoming a curse for us" vs. "he took upon himself the curse for our wrongdoing"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having become a curse for us" | genomenos hyper hēmōn katara |
| LOGOS | "by becoming a curse for us" | Same; instrumental participle |
| PNEUMA | "he took upon himself the curse for our wrongdoing" | "Took upon himself"; adds "for our wrongdoing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says Christ became a curse — not that he "took upon himself" a curse (which softens the identification). PNEUMA also adds "for our wrongdoing" — Paul says simply "for us" (hyper hēmōn), not specifying what "for us" means.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA softens and expands.

---

### v.16 — "seed" vs. "offspring" vs. "child"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his seed...seeds...your seed" | sperma/spermata — literal |
| LOGOS | "his offspring...offsprings...your offspring" | "Offspring" — standard; coined plural |
| PNEUMA | "his child...children...his child" | "Child/children" — relational language |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. sperma means "seed/offspring/descendant." LOGOS uses "offspring" and coins a deliberate pseudo-plural "offsprings" to mirror Paul's argument about the singular vs. plural. PNEUMA uses "child/children" — which works but loses the agricultural/generative connotation of sperma.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Offspring" preserves sperma better than "child."

---

### v.19 — "because of transgressions" — purpose of the law

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It was added because of the transgressions" | tōn parabaseōn charin — "for the sake of" |
| LOGOS | "It was added because of transgressions" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "It was given alongside the promise to show people their sins" | "To show people their sins" — purpose gloss; "alongside the promise" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. charin + genitive means "for the sake of / because of." The exact sense is debated: to restrain transgressions? To provoke them? To identify them? Paul does not specify. PNEUMA chooses "to show people their sins" — one interpretation among several — and adds "alongside the promise" (not in Greek).

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. The purpose of the law (tōn parabaseōn charin) is one of the most debated phrases in Galatians. Paul deliberately does not specify whether the law was given to restrain sin, reveal sin, or provoke sin. PNEUMA's "to show people their sins" is one reading that forecloses the others.

---

### v.22 — "imprisoned everything under sin" vs. "we are all prisoners of sin"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Scripture confined all [things] under sin" | synekleisen hē graphē ta panta — "Scripture confined all things" |
| LOGOS | "the Scripture imprisoned everything under sin" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the Scriptures declare that we are all prisoners of sin" | "Declare" for "imprisoned"; "we" for "everything" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The subject is "the Scripture" (singular, hē graphē) — not "Scriptures" (plural, as PNEUMA). The verb synkleiō means "shut up together/imprison" — not "declare." PNEUMA changes the metaphor from imprisonment to declaration and narrows "everything" (ta panta) to "we."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes verb, subject, and object.

---

### v.23-24 — "guardian" (paidagōgos) rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the law has become our guardian unto Christos" | paidagōgos — "child-leader/guardian" |
| LOGOS | "the law was our guardian until Christ came" | Same — "guardian" |
| PNEUMA | "The law was our guardian until Christ came; it protected us" | Adds "it protected us" as gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. paidagōgos in the ancient world was a slave who escorted a child to school — not a teacher, but a custodian. "Guardian" captures this. PNEUMA adds "it protected us" — one interpretation of the guardian's function, but Paul does not specify protection as the primary role.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds protective function.

---

### v.26 — "sons of God" vs. "children of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you are all sons of God through the faith in Christos Yeshua" | huioi theou — "sons" |
| LOGOS | "you are all sons of God, through faith" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "you are all children of God through faith in Christ Jesus" | "Children" — gender-inclusive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. huioi is specifically "sons" — the legal term for heirs in Roman law. Paul uses huioi rather than tekna ("children") because the context is inheritance and legal standing. "Sons" preserves the legal metaphor. PNEUMA's "children" loses the juridical dimension.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. huioi as legal heirs — "sons" vs. "children."

---

### v.27 — "put on Christos" vs. "put on Christ" vs. "put on Christ, like putting on new clothes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "have put on Christos" | enedysasthe Christon — "clothed yourselves in Christ" |
| LOGOS | "have put on Christ" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "have put on Christ, like putting on new clothes" | Adds simile "like putting on new clothes" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. endyō means "put on/clothe oneself in." The clothing metaphor is inherent in the verb. PNEUMA adds "like putting on new clothes" — making the implicit metaphor explicit, but adding content not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA makes implicit metaphor explicit.

---

### v.28 — "Ioudaios" vs. "Jew" vs. "Jew/Gentile"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "There is not Ioudaios nor Greek" | Transliterated |
| LOGOS | "There is neither Jew nor Greek" | Standard |
| PNEUMA | "There is no longer Jew or Gentile" | "Gentile" for "Greek"; adds "no longer" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says Hellēn (Greek), not ethnē (Gentile/nations). "Greek" is the specific ethnic counterpart to "Jew" — PNEUMA's "Gentile" is broader. PNEUMA also adds "no longer" (ouketi), which is not in the text (the Greek has simply ouk eni, "there is not").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Greek" vs. "Gentile"; "there is not" vs. "no longer."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 7 | Similes, purpose clauses, protective function, "about Christ," "for our wrongdoing" |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 3 | Paraphrases (vv.2, 8, 22), changes subjects and verbs |
| Gender-inclusive rendering | 2 | "Children" for huioi (v.26), "brothers and sisters" (v.15) |
| akoēs pisteōs ambiguity | 1 | "Hearing of/with faith" — relationship between hearing and faith |
| Purpose of the law | 1 | tōn parabaseōn charin — why the law was given (v.19) |
| EMET transliteration | 2 | Ioudaios, Christos |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 3 contains Paul's most sustained theological argument in Galatians, and the translators diverge most on passages where Paul is deliberately compressed or ambiguous — the akoēs pisteōs construction (v.2), the purpose of the law (v.19), and the synkleiō metaphor (v.22). PNEUMA continues to resolve ambiguities Paul left open and to add interpretive content. The most theologically significant disagreements involve the mechanism by which hearing relates to faith and the purpose for which the law was given.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
